news_summary: Today's positive human rights news highlights progress towards environmental rights recognition in Europe, groundbreaking creative expression of protest movements through AI film, and ongoing efforts to strengthen European unity and support for Ukraine and the Western Balkans.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité positive d'aujourd'hui en matière de droits de l'homme met en lumière les progrès accomplis vers la reconnaissance des droits environnementaux en Europe, l'expression créative novatrice des mouvements de protestation à travers un film réalisé par l'IA, ainsi que les efforts continus visant à renforcer l'unité européenne et le soutien apporté à l'Ukraine et aux Balkans occidentaux.
news_summary_es: Las noticias positivas de hoy en materia de derechos humanos destacan los avances hacia el reconocimiento de los derechos medioambientales en Europa, la innovadora expresión creativa de los movimientos de protesta a través de un cortometraje creado con IA, y los esfuerzos continuos por reforzar la unidad europea y el apoyo a Ucrania y a los Balcanes Occidentales.

articles.title: Time for Europe to Recognize the Right to a Healthy Environment
articles.summary: On the eve of World Environment Day, I joined around 400 environmental defenders and policymakers from across Europe in Strasbourg, France, where they gathered at the first European Forum on Environmental Human Rights Defenders. As Karin Kvarfordt Niia, a reindeer herder from Sweden’s Sami community, discussed how mining projects have displaced Sami people from their lands, she noted: “Defenders who raise their voices are portrayed as obstacles.” Europe has been a perhaps surprisingly hostile environment for climate activists, and defenders feel keenly that the lack of a regional human rights instrument explicitly recognizing the right to a clean, healthy and sustainable environment contributes to it. Neither the European Convention on Human Rights nor the European Social Charter, the Council of Europe’s leading rights treaties, explicitly includes the right. Without a specific treaty in which to anchor their advocacy, states and companies more easily dismiss defenders' work as unjustified obstruction: lowering the costs of targeting them and sometimes depriving them of standing in court. If the right to a clean, healthy, and sustainable environment was enshrined in the Convention, defenders would have access to the European Court of Human Rights giving them the opportunity to demonstrate which actions and omissions are violating the right to a healthy environment, and seek a concrete, binding legal remedy from the court. The conditions for Europe to do what is needed have never been more favorable. The International Court of Justice's Advisory Opinion on climate change recognized the right to a healthy environment as a precondition for the enjoyment of all other human rights. Building on the UN General Assembly’s 2022 recognition of the right as universal, last month’s General Assembly resolution welcoming that Opinion shows growing global momentum.African and Inter-American human rights systems have recognized the right to a healthy environment for decades and the Council of Europe’s own Parliamentary Assembly has already set in motion a process to draft the necessary instrument.Environmental defenders across Europe currently operate in a fragmented landscape of uneven national protections and inconsistent international standards. The member states in the Council of Europe need to take this long overdue step urgently: Europe needs an instrument explicitly recognizing the right to a clean, healthy and sustainable environment.
articles.content:
On the eve of World Environment Day, I joined around 400 environmental defenders and policymakers from across Europe in Strasbourg, France, where they gathered at the first European Forum on Environmental Human Rights Defenders. As Karin Kvarfordt Niia, a reindeer herder from Sweden’s Sami community, discussed how mining projects have displaced Sami people from their lands, she noted: “Defenders who raise their voices are portrayed as obstacles.”

Europe has been a perhaps surprisingly hostile environment for climate activists, and defenders feel keenly that the lack of a regional human rights instrument explicitly recognizing the right to a clean, healthy and sustainable environment contributes to it. Neither the European Convention on Human Rights nor the European Social Charter, the Council of Europe’s leading rights treaties, explicitly includes the right.

Without a specific treaty in which to anchor their advocacy, states and companies more easily dismiss defenders' work as unjustified obstruction: lowering the costs of targeting them and sometimes depriving them of standing in court. If the right to a clean, healthy, and sustainable environment was enshrined in the Convention, defenders would have access to the European Court of Human Rights giving them the opportunity to demonstrate which actions and omissions are violating the right to a healthy environment, and seek a concrete, binding legal remedy from the court.

The conditions for Europe to do what is needed have never been more favorable. The International Court of Justice's Advisory Opinion on climate change recognized the right to a healthy environment as a precondition for the enjoyment of all other human rights. Building on the UN General Assembly’s 2022 recognition of the right as universal, last month’s General Assembly resolution welcoming that Opinion shows growing global momentum.

African and Inter-American human rights systems have recognized the right to a healthy environment for decades and the Council of Europe’s own Parliamentary Assembly has already set in motion a process to draft the necessary instrument.

Environmental defenders across Europe currently operate in a fragmented landscape of uneven national protections and inconsistent international standards. The member states in the Council of Europe need to take this long overdue step urgently: Europe needs an instrument explicitly recognizing the right to a clean, healthy and sustainable environment.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a significant and inspiring movement toward legally recognizing the right to a clean, healthy, and sustainable environment across Europe, which would empower environmental defenders and provide concrete legal remedies. This development has broad societal impact, aligns with global momentum, and addresses a critical human rights and environmental issue with detailed context.
articles.personality_title: Europe moves closer to legally recognizing the right to a healthy environment
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Around 400 environmental defenders and policymakers gathered in Strasbourg, France, just before World Environment Day. They met at the first European Forum on Environmental Human Rights Defenders to discuss challenges faced by activists protecting the environment.

**What happened** – The forum highlighted that Europe currently lacks a clear legal right to a clean, healthy, and sustainable environment in its main human rights treaties. This gap makes it easier for governments and companies to dismiss or block environmental defenders. For example, Karin Kvarfordt Niia, a reindeer herder from Sweden, shared how mining projects have pushed her Sami community off their land, while defenders speaking out are often seen as obstacles.

**Impact** – Without a specific legal right, defenders have limited power to challenge harmful actions in court. If Europe officially recognized this right, defenders could use the European Court of Human Rights to hold governments and companies accountable. This would provide stronger protection for people and nature. The timing is favorable because global bodies like the United Nations and the International Court of Justice have recently supported the idea that a healthy environment is essential for all human rights.

**What's next step** – The Council of Europe’s Parliamentary Assembly is working on creating a new legal instrument to recognize this right. European countries need to act quickly to adopt it, helping unify protections across the continent and give defenders a clear legal basis for their work.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Europe is preparing to officially recognize the right to a healthy environment, giving environmental defenders stronger legal tools to protect nature and communities.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'Europe se rapproche de la reconnaissance juridique du droit à un environnement sain
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Environ 400 défenseurs de l'environnement et décideurs se sont réunis à Strasbourg, en France, juste avant la Journée mondiale de l'environnement. Ils ont participé au premier Forum européen des défenseurs des droits environnementaux pour discuter des difficultés rencontrées par les militants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le forum a montré qu’il manque actuellement en Europe un droit clair à un environnement propre, sain et durable dans ses principaux traités de droits humains. Cette absence facilite le rejet ou le blocage des défenseurs par les gouvernements et entreprises. Par exemple, Karin Kvarfordt Niia, éleveuse de rennes en Suède, a raconté comment des projets miniers ont chassé sa communauté Sami de ses terres, tandis que les défenseurs sont souvent vus comme des obstacles.

**Impact** – Sans un droit légal spécifique, les défenseurs ont peu de pouvoir pour contester les actions nuisibles devant les tribunaux. Si l’Europe reconnaît officiellement ce droit, ils pourraient saisir la Cour européenne des droits de l’homme pour tenir les gouvernements et entreprises responsables. Cela offrirait une meilleure protection pour les personnes et la nature. Le moment est favorable, car des organismes mondiaux comme l’ONU et la Cour internationale de justice soutiennent récemment que l’environnement sain est essentiel à tous les droits humains.

**Prochaine étape** – L’Assemblée parlementaire du Conseil de l’Europe travaille à la création d’un nouvel instrument juridique pour reconnaître ce droit. Les pays européens doivent agir rapidement pour l’adopter, afin d’unifier les protections sur le continent et donner aux défenseurs une base légale claire.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – L’Europe se prépare à reconnaître officiellement le droit à un environnement sain, offrant aux défenseurs de l’environnement des outils juridiques renforcés pour protéger la nature et les communautés.
articles.personality_title_es: Europa avanza hacia el reconocimiento legal del derecho a un ambiente saludable
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cerca de 400 defensores ambientales y responsables políticos se reunieron en Estrasburgo, Francia, justo antes del Día Mundial del Medio Ambiente. Se encontraron en el primer Foro Europeo de Defensores de los Derechos Ambientales para hablar sobre los retos que enfrentan los activistas.

**Qué pasó** – El foro mostró que Europa no tiene un derecho claro a un ambiente limpio, saludable y sostenible en sus principales tratados de derechos humanos. Esta falta facilita que gobiernos y empresas rechacen o bloqueen a los defensores. Por ejemplo, Karin Kvarfordt Niia, pastora de renos en Suecia, contó cómo proyectos mineros desplazaron a su comunidad Sami de sus tierras, mientras que los defensores son vistos como obstáculos.

**Impacto** – Sin un derecho legal específico, los defensores tienen poco poder para desafiar acciones dañinas en los tribunales. Si Europa reconociera oficialmente este derecho, podrían acudir al Tribunal Europeo de Derechos Humanos para responsabilizar a gobiernos y empresas. Esto ofrecería mayor protección para las personas y la naturaleza. El momento es favorable porque organismos globales como la ONU y la Corte Internacional de Justicia han apoyado recientemente que un ambiente saludable es clave para todos los derechos humanos.

**Próximo paso** – La Asamblea Parlamentaria del Consejo de Europa está trabajando para crear un nuevo instrumento legal que reconozca este derecho. Los países europeos deben actuar rápido para adoptarlo, unificando las protecciones y dando a los defensores una base legal clara.

**Resumen en una frase** – Europa está lista para reconocer oficialmente el derecho a un ambiente saludable, brindando a los defensores ambientales herramientas legales más fuertes para proteger la naturaleza y las comunidades.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a glowing, ancient reindeer with intricate Sami patterns on its fur standing protectively on a lush European landscape, surrounded by softly glowing silhouettes of diverse human figures holding hands in a circle beneath a sky where a balanced scale made of natural elements gently floats, symbolizing justice and the right to a healthy environment.

articles.title: AI feature film on Iran’s protest movement makes festival history
articles.summary: A film about Iran's protest movement is making cinema history. "Dreams of Violets" is the first fully AI-generated feature film ever selected by a major international film festival. The 75-minute drama will premiere at New York's Tribeca Festival next week. Created by Iranian-British director Ash Koosha from his home in London, the film took just three months to produce and cost less than 2,000 euros. There were no actors, no cameras, no sets and no film crew. Koosha says the film simply could not have been made through conventional means. Living in exile and unable to safely film inside Iran, he turned to AI to recreate events linked to the country's deadly crackdown on anti-government protesters. The film is based on journalistic reports, photographs and eyewitness accounts, and explores themes of memory, censorship and resistance. But as Tribeca becomes the first major festival to embrace a fully AI-generated feature, the film is also reigniting a fierce debate. Can artificial intelligence tell deeply human stories? Does AI democratise filmmaking or threaten the future of the industry? Eve Jackson speaks to Ash Koosha about Iran, ethics and the future of cinema.
articles.content:
AI feature film on Iran’s protest movement makes festival history

Culture

To display this content from YouTube, you must enable advertisement tracking and audience measurement. Accept Manage my choices One of your browser extensions seems to be blocking the video player from loading. To watch this content, you may need to disable it on this site. Try again ARTS24 © FRANCE 24 12:38

12:38 min Share

Play (12:38 min)

From the show

Reading time 1 min

A film about Iran's protest movement is making cinema history. "Dreams of Violets" is the first fully AI-generated feature film ever selected by a major international film festival. The 75-minute drama will premiere at New York's Tribeca Festival next week. Created by Iranian-British director Ash Koosha from his home in London, the film took just three months to produce and cost less than 2,000 euros. There were no actors, no cameras, no sets and no film crew. Koosha says the film simply could not have been made through conventional means. Living in exile and unable to safely film inside Iran, he turned to AI to recreate events linked to the country's deadly crackdown on anti-government protesters. The film is based on journalistic reports, photographs and eyewitness accounts, and explores themes of memory, censorship and resistance. But as Tribeca becomes the first major festival to embrace a fully AI-generated feature, the film is also reigniting a fierce debate. Can artificial intelligence tell deeply human stories? Does AI democratise filmmaking or threaten the future of the industry? Eve Jackson speaks to Ash Koosha about Iran, ethics and the future of cinema.
articles.source: News and stories on CULTURE, movies, music, literature - France 24
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a groundbreaking AI-generated film about Iran's protest movement, highlighting a new way to document and share stories of resistance and human rights under censorship. It has broad significance as it enables storytelling from exile and raises important ethical and societal questions about AI in art and activism.
articles.personality_title: AI-generated film about Iran protests premieres at Tribeca Festival
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Iran has seen protests against the government, but filming these events inside the country is dangerous. Ash Koosha, an Iranian-British director living in London, wanted to tell this story but could not safely shoot a traditional movie.

**What happened** – Koosha created "Dreams of Violets," a 75-minute drama that is the first feature film made entirely using artificial intelligence (AI). The film was produced in just three months with a budget under 2,000 euros. It has no actors, cameras, or sets. Instead, AI technology was used to recreate scenes based on news reports, photos, and witness stories about Iran’s crackdown on protesters. The film will premiere at the Tribeca Festival in New York.

**Impact** – This film is unique because it shows how AI can help tell important stories when traditional filmmaking is impossible. It opens new ways for people in exile or under censorship to share their experiences. At the same time, it raises questions about whether AI can capture human emotions and how it might change the film industry.

**What's next step** – The film’s premiere at a major festival could encourage more artists to use AI for storytelling. It also sparks discussions among filmmakers and audiences about the ethics and future role of AI in creating movies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – "Dreams of Violets" is the first fully AI-made feature film, using technology to share a powerful story about Iran’s protests while challenging ideas about filmmaking’s future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un film entièrement généré par IA sur les protestations en Iran présenté au Festival de Tribeca
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En Iran, filmer les manifestations contre le gouvernement est dangereux. Ash Koosha, réalisateur irano-britannique vivant à Londres, souhaitait raconter cette histoire mais ne pouvait pas tourner un film traditionnel en toute sécurité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Koosha a créé « Rêves de Violettes », un drame de 75 minutes entièrement réalisé grâce à l’intelligence artificielle (IA). Le film a été produit en seulement trois mois avec un budget inférieur à 2 000 euros. Il n’y a ni acteurs, ni caméras, ni décors. La technologie IA a recréé des scènes basées sur des reportages, photos et témoignages liés à la répression des manifestants en Iran. Le film sera présenté au Festival de Tribeca à New York.

**Impact** – Ce film est unique car il montre comment l’IA peut aider à raconter des histoires importantes quand le cinéma traditionnel est impossible. Il ouvre de nouvelles possibilités pour ceux qui vivent en exil ou sous censure de partager leurs expériences. En même temps, il soulève des questions sur la capacité de l’IA à exprimer des émotions humaines et sur l’avenir du cinéma.

**Prochaine étape** – La projection au festival pourrait encourager d’autres artistes à utiliser l’IA pour raconter des histoires. Cela lance aussi un débat sur l’éthique et le rôle futur de l’IA dans la création de films.

**Résumé en une phrase** – « Rêves de Violettes » est le premier long-métrage entièrement créé par IA, utilisant la technologie pour raconter une histoire forte sur les protestations en Iran tout en posant des questions sur l’avenir du cinéma.
articles.personality_title_es: Película generada por IA sobre protestas en Irán se estrena en el Festival de Tribeca
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Irán, filmar las protestas contra el gobierno es peligroso. Ash Koosha, director iraní-británico que vive en Londres, quería contar esta historia pero no podía grabar una película tradicional de forma segura.

**Qué pasó** – Koosha creó "Sueños de Violetas", un drama de 75 minutos hecho completamente con inteligencia artificial (IA). La película se produjo en solo tres meses con un presupuesto menor a 2,000 euros. No hay actores, cámaras ni escenarios; la IA recreó escenas basadas en informes periodísticos, fotos y testimonios sobre la represión a los manifestantes en Irán. La película se estrenará en el Festival de Tribeca en Nueva York.

**Impacto** – Esta película es única porque muestra cómo la IA puede ayudar a contar historias importantes cuando el cine tradicional no es posible. Abre nuevas formas para que personas en el exilio o bajo censura compartan sus experiencias. También plantea preguntas sobre si la IA puede captar emociones humanas y cómo cambiará la industria del cine.

**Próximo paso** – El estreno en un festival importante podría motivar a más artistas a usar IA para contar historias. También genera debates sobre la ética y el futuro papel de la IA en la creación cinematográfica.

**Resumen en una frase** – "Sueños de Violetas" es el primer largometraje totalmente hecho con IA, usando tecnología para contar una historia poderosa sobre las protestas en Irán mientras cuestiona el futuro del cine.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing violet flower blooming amidst a stylized, abstract cityscape silhouette representing Tehran, with soft, flowing lines symbolizing digital data streams weaving through the scene, conveying memory, resistance, and the fusion of human stories with AI creation.

articles.title: Ukraine, the Western Balkans and the EU’s Enlargement agenda: Interview with MEP David McAllister
articles.summary: On Wednesday, June 3, the European Newsroom sat down with German MEP David McAllister to talk about the opportunities and challenges ahead for countries aspiring to join the EU – in particular Ukraine and the Western Balkans.
articles.content:
On Wednesday, June 3, the European Newsroom sat down with German MEP David McAllister to talk about the opportunities and challenges ahead for countries aspiring to join the EU – in particular Ukraine and the Western Balkans.

In an interview with the European Newsroom (enr), the leading German foreign policy politician in the European Parliament, David McAllister, urged Ukraine to accept a recent proposal by German Chancellor Friedrich Merz for an “associate membership” in the EU.

McAllister, who sits with the European People’s Party (EPP) and chairs the European Parliament’s Committee on Foreign Affairs (AFET), told the enr he is in favour of enhanced, step-by-step integration of Ukraine into the EU in order to make the benefits of the EU visible to the Ukrainian people. One example was for Ukraine to enter the European Parliament as an observer.

Kyiv had previously rejected the idea of an “associate membership”. In a letter to EU leaders, President Volodymyr Zelenskyy wrote that it would be unfair for Ukraine to have a presence in the EU but remain without a voice. Instead, he urged the EU to move faster and not keep Ukraine in a waiting room.

Ukraine is not the only country in line for EU membership. Eight other European nations are currently also recognised candidates to join the bloc: Albania, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Georgia, Moldova, Montenegro, North Macedonia, Serbia, Türkiye.

McAllister told the enr that enlargement policy is at the top of the EU agenda and that the Western Balkan countries are at the heart of Europe. As such, a stable region is an investment in peace and security, he said.

At the same time, the German politician advocated for an individual approach to each country in the region based on merit, rather than grouping them together as one. He explained that the countries of the Western Balkans are individually at very different stages of progress. As an example, he pointed out that Montenegro is a clear frontrunner, while North Macedonia’s EU path remains blocked due to a political deadlock with Bulgaria.

The interview was conducted by journalists from European Newsroom partner agenciesdpa (Germany), CTK (Czech Republic), and Tanjug (Serbia) on the behalf of the entire network.

Read some of the coverage from our network here:
articles.reasoning: The article discusses the EU's enlargement agenda focusing on Ukraine and Western Balkans, highlighting efforts towards political integration, peace, security, and democratic progress. This has broad significance for regional stability and human rights advancement, with concrete proposals and political dialogue detailed.
articles.personality_title: EU plans closer ties with Ukraine and Western Balkans to strengthen peace and progress
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Several countries in Europe want to join the European Union (EU). Ukraine and nations in the Western Balkans are among these. Joining the EU means becoming part of a group that works together on politics, economy, and security.

**What happened** – On June 3, German Member of the European Parliament (MEP) David McAllister talked about new ideas to help Ukraine and Western Balkan countries move closer to the EU. He supports a step-by-step plan, including letting Ukraine become an “associate member” first. This means Ukraine would have some rights in the EU, like observing meetings, but not full membership yet. Other countries like Albania, Serbia, and Montenegro are also waiting to join.

**Impact** – This approach shows the EU wants to build trust and bring benefits to these countries gradually. It helps people in Ukraine and the Balkans see the advantages of joining the EU, such as peace and better cooperation. McAllister also said it’s important to treat each country based on its own progress, since they are all different. For example, Montenegro is ahead, while North Macedonia faces political problems.

**What's next step** – The EU will continue talks with these countries and decide how to support each one’s path to membership. Ukraine’s idea of associate membership will be discussed more, while the EU also keeps working on resolving issues like the one between North Macedonia and Bulgaria.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The EU is working on a careful plan to include Ukraine and Western Balkan countries, aiming for peace and progress by supporting each nation’s unique journey toward membership.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’UE renforce ses liens avec l’Ukraine et les Balkans occidentaux pour plus de paix et de progrès
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Plusieurs pays d’Europe souhaitent rejoindre l’Union européenne (UE). L’Ukraine et les pays des Balkans occidentaux en font partie. Rejoindre l’UE signifie faire partie d’un groupe qui collabore en politique, économie et sécurité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 3 juin, le député européen allemand David McAllister a parlé de nouvelles idées pour aider l’Ukraine et les pays des Balkans occidentaux à se rapprocher de l’UE. Il soutient un plan progressif, incluant l’adhésion « associée » pour l’Ukraine. Cela signifie que l’Ukraine aurait certains droits dans l’UE, comme observer les réunions, sans être membre à part entière. D’autres pays comme l’Albanie, la Serbie et le Monténégro attendent aussi de rejoindre.

**Impact** – Cette approche montre que l’UE veut gagner la confiance et offrir des avantages progressivement. Cela aide les populations d’Ukraine et des Balkans à voir les bénéfices de l’adhésion, comme la paix et une meilleure coopération. McAllister a aussi dit qu’il est important de traiter chaque pays selon ses progrès, car ils sont différents. Par exemple, le Monténégro est en avance, alors que la Macédoine du Nord a des problèmes politiques.

**Prochaine étape** – L’UE continuera les discussions avec ces pays pour soutenir leur chemin vers l’adhésion. Le projet d’adhésion associée pour l’Ukraine sera étudié davantage, tandis que l’UE travaillera aussi à résoudre des problèmes comme celui entre la Macédoine du Nord et la Bulgarie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’UE prépare un plan progressif pour intégrer l’Ukraine et les Balkans occidentaux, visant la paix et le progrès en soutenant le parcours propre à chaque pays vers l’adhésion.
articles.personality_title_es: La UE fortalece lazos con Ucrania y los Balcanes Occidentales para impulsar paz y progreso
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Varios países de Europa quieren unirse a la Unión Europea (UE). Ucrania y los países de los Balcanes Occidentales están entre ellos. Unirse a la UE significa formar parte de un grupo que trabaja junto en política, economía y seguridad.

**Qué pasó** – El 3 de junio, el eurodiputado alemán David McAllister habló sobre nuevas ideas para ayudar a Ucrania y a los países de los Balcanes Occidentales a acercarse a la UE. Apoya un plan paso a paso, que incluye que Ucrania sea miembro “asociado” primero. Esto significa que Ucrania tendría algunos derechos en la UE, como observar reuniones, pero no la membresía completa. Otros países como Albania, Serbia y Montenegro también esperan unirse.

**Impacto** – Este enfoque muestra que la UE quiere construir confianza y ofrecer beneficios poco a poco. Esto ayuda a la gente en Ucrania y los Balcanes a ver las ventajas de unirse, como la paz y mejor cooperación. McAllister también dijo que es importante tratar a cada país según su propio progreso, porque son diferentes. Por ejemplo, Montenegro está más avanzado, mientras que Macedonia del Norte enfrenta problemas políticos.

**Próximo paso** – La UE continuará las conversaciones con estos países para apoyar su camino hacia la membresía. La idea de membresía asociada para Ucrania se discutirá más, y la UE seguirá trabajando para resolver problemas como el de Macedonia del Norte y Bulgaria.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La UE trabaja en un plan cuidadoso para incluir a Ucrania y los Balcanes Occidentales, buscando paz y progreso al apoyar el camino único de cada nación hacia la membresía.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a radiant golden key unlocking a large, ornate gate shaped like the European Union flag, with a glowing path leading forward where stylized symbolic figures representing Ukraine and Western Balkan countries—depicted as diverse, colorful puzzle pieces—move step-by-step toward the open gate under a calm sky of soft blues and gentle earth tones.
